summary: Security and trade pressures are converging on Mexico's government. Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo was detained in a fuel-theft, organized-crime probe, arriving as Sheinbaum faces US scrutiny over Morena governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora suspected of cartel ties. On trade, US negotiators head to Mexico City for a third USMCA review round, while Chinese-made trucks operating in Mexico complicate efforts to ease Trump tariffs, and Banxico's Heath warns inflation risks still tilt upward.

lead.h1: Former Baja California governor Ruffo detained in cartel-linked case
lead.context: The arrest of the first opposition governor in recent memory lands amid US scrutiny of Morena governors' alleged cartel ties, reshaping the political fight over organized crime.

items.headline: US and Mexico prepare third round of USMCA review talks
items.context: A US negotiating team will travel to Mexico City to discuss steel, autos and trade as both countries work toward the joint review of the USMCA.
items.background: The USMCA joint review process, now conducted annually after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, is being supplemented by bilateral negotiating rounds between the US and Mexico on specific trade issues.
items.drivers: USTR is sending its negotiating team to Mexico City for a third bilateral round from July 21 to 23, covering steel and aluminum, autos, economic security, labor, agriculture and electronic payment services.
items.implications: USTR representative Jamieson Greer credited Economy Secretary Marcelo Ebrard's government with cooperation, suggesting the bilateral track is advancing separately from the broader treaty review.
items.next: The two delegations meet in Mexico City from July 21 to 23.

items.headline: Chinese-made heavy trucks in Mexico complicate bid to ease Trump tariffs
items.context: A growing fleet of China-built trucks operating in Mexico gives Washington another argument to maintain tariff pressure, tying trade relief to scrutiny of Chinese content in Mexico's supply chains.
items.background: The USMCA is under annual review after the US declined a 16-year extension in July 2026, with Chinese content in Mexican supply chains a central sticking point.
items.drivers: Chinese truck import value into Mexico rose more than sevenfold over six years and the number of Chinese truck firms operating there reached about 23, alarming US officials and Mexican manufacturers.
items.implications: The trend gives Washington grounds to keep tariff pressure on Mexico's trucking sector while Mexican negotiators try to show alignment against Chinese inflows.

items.headline: Grupo Carso buys TotalEnergies' 30% stake in a Gulf of Mexico oil block
items.context: Carlos Slim's conglomerate expands into offshore oil exploration, adding private capital to a sector still dominated by Pemex.
items.background: Pemex remains the dominant player in Mexican oil, but private firms operate blocks alongside it under contracts and joint ventures in the Gulf of Mexico.
items.drivers: TotalEnergies is exiting Block 30, and Carso is expanding into segments of the oil and gas sector not under state control, following its earlier purchase of Fieldwood Mexico from Lukoil.
items.implications: The deal extends Slim's holdings in a field operated by Harbour Energy, adding private capital to offshore exploration outside Pemex's direct control.
items.next: The transaction requires approval from Mexican authorities.

items.headline: Banxico deputy governor says inflation risks remain tilted to the upside
items.context: Jonathan Heath said the balance of risks has not turned favorable despite June's slowdown, a signal the central bank may hold off on further rate cuts.
items.background: Banxico targets 3% inflation, give or take one percentage point, and adjusts its policy rate to hold inflation near that goal.
items.drivers: Heath pointed to better-than-expected inflation in the second half of June but said it remains unclear whether the decline will hold through the rest of the year.
items.implications: His comment that the balance of risks is not yet favorable suggests Banxico may proceed cautiously on further rate moves.

items.headline: Sheinbaum faces scrutiny over Morena governors' alleged cartel ties
items.context: Reports point to governors in Baja California, Sinaloa, Tamaulipas and Sonora facing US scrutiny over suspected links to organized crime, testing the government's response as it downplays the allegations.

items.headline: Former Baja California governor Ernesto Ruffo arrested on organized crime charges
items.context: Prosecutors said the arrest, tied to a fuel-theft investigation into his company, followed a high-complexity probe; a business partner was also detained.
items.background: Baja California's first opposition governor after decades of one-party rule, Ruffo led the state from 1989 to 1995. Fuel theft, known as huachicol, has long involved diversion of imported fuel through customs and rail schemes that evade taxes.
items.drivers: The FGR describes a high-complexity investigation into Ingemar, a company Ruffo founded that he has said only handled customs paperwork, leading to his arrest along with business partner Ricardo Thompson Navarro.
items.implications: A former governor now faces organized crime and tax-fuel-smuggling charges, tying a prominent political figure to an alleged contraband network.
items.next: Ruffo has been transferred, handcuffed, to FGR facilities in Tijuana.

items.headline: Pemex halts oil exports to Cuba for first time in 33 years
items.context: The state oil company stops shipments after Trump administration warnings, a move that signals Mexico's caution in handling energy ties with Havana under US pressure.
items.background: Pemex is Mexico's state oil company, financially intertwined with the federal government and a longtime crude and fuel exporter, including to Cuba.
items.drivers: The halt follows sustained pressure from the Trump administration, which has threatened to intervene against Cuba's government.
items.implications: Exports to Cuba fell to zero in May, the first such stoppage since records began in 1993, showing Mexico's caution in balancing US pressure against its own foreign-policy stance.
items.next: Sheinbaum said in late June that Mexico will keep sending petroleum products despite Trump's threats.

standing.text: The peso trades at 17.52 pesos to the dollar; inflation is 3.37%; the policy rate is 6.50%.

live.tmpl: the peso trades at {v} pesos to the dollar
